era_context: Early Bronze Age III; Old Kingdom Egypt (Dynasty IV–V transition); Early Dynastic III Mesopotamia; Mature Indus Valley Civilization. This year likely marks the end of Menkaure's reign or the early years of his successor Shepseskaf, depending on the chronological system used.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia outside the Yellow River valley

events.title: End of Menkaure's reign or reign of Shepseskaf (Egypt, Dynasty IV)
events.description: This year likely marks the end of Menkaure's reign and the beginning of his successor Shepseskaf's rule, or falls within the final years of Menkaure's reign, depending on the chronological system. Shepseskaf is attested as the last pharaoh of Dynasty IV. Unlike his predecessors, Shepseskaf did not construct a pyramid; instead, he built a mastaba (a rectangular tomb structure) at Saqqara, marking a significant shift in royal burial practices and possibly reflecting changes in state ideology or resources. This transition marks the end of the great pyramid-building age of Dynasty IV and the beginning of Dynasty V, which saw a shift in royal priorities toward sun temples and a relative decline in monumental tomb construction.
key_figures: Menkaure (Pharaoh, Dynasty IV, possibly ending this year)
Shepseskaf (Pharaoh, Dynasty IV, possibly beginning this year)

sources.name: Egyptian king-list tradition (Abydos King List, Turin Papyrus)

events.certainty_note: The transition from Menkaure to Shepseskaf is well-attested in king-lists, but the specific year is uncertain. Conventional chronology places Menkaure's reign c. 2490–2472 BCE and Shepseskaf's c. 2472–2467 BCE, but alternative chronologies shift these dates significantly. The year -2940 (2941 BCE) is assigned here as an approximate date for this transition, but it could be off by several years depending on the chronological system.

events.title: Indus Valley Civilization — Mature phase continues
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization remains in its mature phase. Urban centers, trade networks, and agricultural production continue without documented major disruptions. The civilization is at its peak in terms of urban development and population.
events.certainty_note: The mature Indus Valley Civilization is archaeologically well-attested for this period.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the transition from Menkaure to Shepseskaf is subject to significant chronological debate. The conventional Egyptian chronology places this transition c. 2472 BCE, but alternative chronologies (particularly the 'low chronology') would place it 50–100+ years later. Additionally, some scholars debate whether Shepseskaf was truly the last pharaoh of Dynasty IV or whether there were additional rulers between him and the beginning of Dynasty V. The significance of Shepseskaf's shift from pyramid to mastaba construction is also debated: some scholars interpret it as a reflection of declining state resources or a change in royal ideology, while others suggest it may have been a deliberate choice reflecting religious or political considerations.
historiographic_note: The historical record for this year is heavily dependent on Egyptian king-lists, which are themselves compilations made centuries or millennia after the events they describe. The transition from Dynasty IV to Dynasty V is one of the more significant chronological markers in Old Kingdom history, but the precise dating remains uncertain. Radiocarbon dating has introduced additional uncertainty into the conventional chronology. For the Indus Valley, the absence of deciphered writing means that political events are invisible; only material culture and settlement patterns are available.

graph_edges.note: Transition from Dynasty IV to Dynasty V; shift in royal burial practices and state priorities
End of the Dynasty IV period that began with Menkaure
